headline: HOGS EXPLODE 22% AS WHEAT DROPS 22¢
subheadline: Disease fears trigger historic hog rally while grain complex softens ahead of planting season.
lead: Lean hogs exploded 22.1% to $105.20 in overnight trading — the kind of move that happens maybe twice a decade — while Chicago wheat dropped 22¢ to $5.97/bu on improving global supply prospects. Corn eased 5½¢ to $4.53/bu and soybeans slipped 6¢ to $11.67/bu as the market shifts focus from South American harvest delays to Midwest planting conditions. With optimal corn planting just two weeks away, weather will soon trump everything else.
teaser: Historic hog rally meets grain weakness as planting season approaches.

one_number.context: This 22% overnight surge represents the largest single-day hog move in years, likely driven by disease concerns limiting supply. For livestock producers, this validates recent expansion decisions and suggests strong margins ahead. For grain farmers, it means feed demand remains robust even as grain prices ease.

sections.title: GRAINS & OILSEEDS
sections.body: Corn dropped 5½¢ to $4.53/bu while soybeans slipped 6¢ to $11.67/bu as the market pivots from South American weather stories to Midwest planting preparations. December corn fell 5¢ to $4.80/bu, suggesting the market sees current weakness as temporary. Wheat led losses with a sharp 22¢ drop to $5.97/bu on reports of improving Black Sea export prospects and better spring wheat planting conditions. The grain complex is entering its most weather-sensitive period — every storm system between now and July 4th matters more than export sales.
sections.bottom_line: Grains ease as focus shifts from South America to Midwest weather.
sections.farmer_action: Hold current pricing positions. Weather premium building season starts in two weeks — don't get caught short if conditions turn.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK & DAIRY
sections.body: Lean hogs exploded 22.1% to $105.20 in the most dramatic overnight move seen in years, likely triggered by fresh disease concerns or major supply disruption news. Live cattle gained 0.4% to $244.23 while feeders added 0.5% to $368.12, suggesting the protein complex remains fundamentally strong. Class III milk jumped 2.7% to $17.92 as dairy demand continues outpacing production growth. Soybean meal held steady at $317.90, but with hog margins this explosive, feed demand should remain robust despite higher costs.
sections.bottom_line: Historic hog rally signals tight protein supplies across the board.
sections.farmer_action: Hog producers: Lock profits immediately on this rally. Cattle feeders: Monitor feed costs but margins remain healthy.

sections.title: ENERGY & INPUTS
sections.body: WTI crude dropped 3.8% to $99.04 as geopolitical tensions eased, providing the first input cost relief farmers have seen in weeks. Natural gas fell 2.3% to $2.82, keeping nitrogen fertilizer costs in check for spring applications. Soybean oil tumbled 2.4% to $67.01 as renewable diesel margins compress, breaking the biodiesel-driven rally that's supported oil prices. Lower energy costs couldn't come at a better time with corn planting just two weeks away — every dollar saved on diesel stretches already-tight operating margins.
sections.bottom_line: Energy costs finally ease as planting season approaches.
sections.farmer_action: Lock diesel prices now if you haven't already — this relief may be temporary with summer driving season ahead.

sections.title: MACRO & TRADE
sections.body: The dollar held steady at $100.00 while gold surged 2.0% to $4,802 — a classic flight-to-quality move suggesting underlying economic uncertainty. The S&P 500 gained 0.7% to $6,575.32, but the gold rally tells the real story about investor nerves. Currency stability helps U.S. export competitiveness just as South American harvest delays create brief windows for additional sales. However, the macro environment remains fragile with inflation concerns keeping Fed policy unpredictable — exactly the kind of backdrop that makes agricultural assets attractive hedges.
sections.bottom_line: Stable dollar helps exports while gold signals macro uncertainty.
sections.farmer_action: No immediate currency hedging needed, but watch for dollar strength that could hurt export demand.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Hog Price Explosions Happen
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 22% hog rally illustrates how thin livestock markets can become. Unlike grains with global production buffers, protein markets operate on razor-thin margins with limited storage. A single disease outbreak, processing plant closure, or export surge can instantly flip supply/demand balance. The hog cycle typically runs 2-3 years from breeding decisions to market, so today's shortage often reflects decisions made when prices were much lower. This volatility is why successful livestock producers focus on risk management, not just production efficiency.

watch_list.desc: Weather models for corn planting conditions across Iowa, Illinois, and Indiana over the next 10 days.
Hog market follow-through — was today's move fundamental or purely technical? Look for supply disruption news.
USDA World Agricultural Supply and Demand Estimates — first official look at 2026/27 crop projections.
Crude oil direction — any sustained move below $95 or above $105 changes the input cost equation significantly.

daily_quote.text: Those too lazy to plow in the right season will have no food at the harvest.

source_summary: CME Group · NYMEX · USDA · Federal Reserve · Open-Meteo
